hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz A207 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the A207 E-Class Cabriolet — from the 1.8-litre turbo four to the 5.5-litre V8. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM651 diesel that redefined the open-top economy equation. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz A207 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the A207 cabriolet specifically — across the full 2010–2017 production run, from the entry-level E 200 CGI to the range-topping E 500.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz A207 is the four-seat cabriolet variant of the fourth-generation E-Class, launched in 2010 as the successor to the A209 CLK-Class Cabriolet. Based on the W204 C-Class platform but sharing 60% of its parts with the W212 E-Class sedan, the A207 brought a fully automatic fabric roof that could be opened or closed in 20 seconds at speeds up to 40 km/h. Engine choice spanned from the 1.8-litre M271 turbo four through the M276 V6 and OM651 diesel range to the M278 twin-turbo V8 in the E 500, with a mid-life facelift in 2013 introducing updated engines including the M274 2.0-litre four and the twin-turbo M276 E400.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W204 (A207 cabriolet variant) • Predecessor: CLK-Class (A209) • Successor: A238 • Sibling body styles in this generation: C207 Coupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The A207 cabriolet attracts strong specialist interest in the UK used market, with OM651 diesel and M276 V6 enquiries dominating pre-purchase inspections [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The A207's engine range splits into four distinct reliability tiers. At the top sits the OM651 2.1-litre diesel (E 220 CDI/E 250 CDI) — a proven workhorse with strong fuel economy, though early examples (pre-2012) can suffer from timing chain tensioner oil leaks and piezo injector issues. The M276 3.5-litre V6 (E 350) is the pick of the petrols — smooth, refined and robust, though coolant leaks from the thermostat housing are documented. The M274 2.0-litre turbo (E 200/E 250, 2013-on) offers a significant reliability improvement over the M271 it replaced. The M271 1.8-litre turbo (E 200 CGI/E 250 CGI) sits in the middle — strong performers but documented timing chain stretch and camshaft sprocket wear. The M273 5.5-litre V8 (E 500, pre-2011) is the least recommended — complex, with intake manifold and timing chain issues, replaced by the much-improved M278 twin-turbo 4.7-litre V8 from 2011. For most buyers, the OM651 diesel or M276 V6 represent the sweet spot between performance, economy and running costs. Who it's for: buyers who want the sweet spot between open-top motoring and running-cost sanity.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain stretch, camshaft sprocket wear

Oil cooler seal leak, swirl flap motor

economicsRule.title: The A207 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,000 M276 recon against a £8,000–£15,000 A207 sits at 20–62% of the car's value. On a sub-£8,000 example, that approaches our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 25–42% — a repair decision, not a financial one. For OM651 diesels, the economics are even more favourable — a £3,000 recon on a £10,000 example is just 30%. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A207 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM651 diesel — strong fuel economy, proven durability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A207 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M273 V8 (pre-2011 E 500) — complex, expensive parts, specialist-only.

keyTakeaways.question: Which A207 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 350 (M276 V6) — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain Stretch and Camshaft Sprocket Wear
problems.rootCause: The M271's timing chain tensioner can fail, allowing the chain to stretch and the camshaft sprockets to wear. Cold-start rattle is the classic early warning — if ignored, the chain can skip, causing catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the timing chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,600 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare A207 M271 timing chain repair quotes →

problems.title: OM651 Timing Chain Tensioner Oil Leak (Recall)
problems.affectedModels: E 220 CDI, E 250 CDI (OM651) — 2010–2014 production (recall issued January 2015).
problems.rootCause: The seal ring between the timing chain tensioner and the engine block can leak oil — this could result in a hazard for other road users and, in extreme cases, chain tensioner loosening and chain failure. A recall was issued in January 2015 for affected vehicles.
problems.recommendation: Check the recall status of any OM651-engined A207 before purchase — if the timing chain tensioner recall hasn't been performed, treat it as immediate preventative maintenance.

cta.label: Compare A207 OM651 timing chain repair quotes →

problems.title: M276 V6 Coolant Leaks (Thermostat Housing)
problems.rootCause: The M276's thermostat housing and coolant pipes can develop leaks, leading to coolant loss and potential overheating if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels regularly — a £400–£800 thermostat housing replacement is cheap insurance against a £2,000+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare A207 M276 coolant system repair quotes →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (V6 Diesel)
problems.rootCause: The OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seals (located in the "V" of the engine) degrade over time, leading to oil leaks that can drip onto the exhaust and cause smoke. Labour-intensive to replace due to engine access.
problems.recommendation: Have the oil cooler seals inspected at every major service — a £700–£1,200 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows oil starvation.

cta.label: Compare A207 OM642 oil cooler seal repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: A207 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz A207 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned A207 engine typically costs between £1,800 and £9,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the volume OM651 diesel sits at £2,800–£4,800, the M276 V6 at £3,000–£5,500, and the M278 V8 in the E 500 at £5,000–£9,000. Fitting adds 8–22 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £520–£1,430 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz A207 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM651 2.1-litre diesel (E 220 CDI/E 250 CDI) is widely regarded as the most durable A207 engine — strong fuel economy, proven longevity and well-documented service requirements. Among petrols, the M276 3.5-litre V6 (E 350, 2011-on) is the pick — smooth, refined and robust, though coolant leaks from the thermostat housing are documented.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz A207?
items.answer: Timing chain stretch and camshaft sprocket wear on the M271 1.8-litre turbo (E 200 CGI/E 250 CGI), timing chain tensioner oil leaks on early OM651 diesels (recall issued January 2015), coolant leaks from the M276 V6 thermostat housing, and oil cooler seal leaks on the OM642 V6 diesel (E 350 CDI) are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz A207 reliable?
items.answer: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The OM651 diesel and M276 V6 are generally reliable with routine maintenance. The M271 family requires careful timing chain history checking. The M272 V6 (pre-2011 E 350) can suffer from balance shaft wear. The M273 V8 (pre-2011 E 500) is complex and expensive to maintain — the M278 twin-turbo V8 that replaced it is a significant improvement. Overall, a well-maintained A207 is a solid used proposition.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz A207 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The M273 5.5-litre V8 (pre-2011 E 500) is the one we consistently advise against — intake manifold issues, timing chain problems and high running costs make it a costly proposition. The M272 V6 (pre-2011 E 350 CGI) can suffer from balance shaft wear on early examples. The M271 is a strong performer but only with documented timing chain history.

items.question: How do I know if my A207 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Cold-start rattle (M271 timing chain), oil leaks from the OM651 timing chain tensioner, coolant loss (M276 thermostat housing), oil leaks from the OM642 oil cooler seals (smoke from under bonnet), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an A207 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £12,000 E 350, a £4,500 M276 recon is 37.5% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £6,000 example, the same recon is 75% — borderline, and we'd advise walking away. For OM651 diesels, the economics are more favourable — a £3,500 recon on a £10,000 example is just 35%. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz A207 engines last?
items.answer: The OM651 diesel can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The M276 V6 petrol is similarly durable, though coolant system maintenance is key. The M271's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can reach 120,000+ miles. The M278 twin-turbo V8 is robust but demands specialist attention.

items.question: What is the difference between the A207 and C207?
items.answer: Both are first-generation E-Class coupé/cabriolet models sharing the W204 platform and most engines. The C207 is the 2-door coupé (2009–2017), while the A207 is the 2-door cabriolet (2010–2017). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the C207 generation page for specifics. The cabriolet's fabric roof mechanism can also add maintenance costs.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz A207 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on M271/M274/M272/M276/M273/M278/OM651/OM642 units. Tell us your variant and approximate year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
